one_line_takeaway: A 210-benchmark meta-review finds that 81% of AI safety benchmarks only test predefined/known risks and 79% reduce safety to unqualified binary pass/fail rates, and proposes ten recommendations grounded in safety engineering and measurement theory to fix construct coverage, risk quantification, and validity gaps.
key_finding: Of the 210 surveyed benchmarks: 81% (N=170) test only 'known known' risks (predefined risk types, fixed triggers) while known-unknowns get limited attention (16%, N=33), unknown-knowns are largely ignored (2%, N=5), and unknown-unknowns are nearly absent (1%, N=2). 79% (N=166) reduce safety evaluation to binary pass/fail rates presented without confidence intervals or uncertainty modeling. Only 34% (N=72) of benchmarks explicitly document which risk types they exclude. Of the 210, only 36 distinguish harm-severity levels at all, and of those only 14 give a principled justification for the severity scale (rest rely on ad hoc author judgment or LLM-generated labels). Only 38% (N=79) ground their metric definitions in an external framework, regulation, or standard. 89% (N=186) evaluate on fixed data without documenting sources of stochasticity (seeds, prompt/judge versioning), undermining precision claims. 68% (N=143) rely on isolated single-turn interactions, diverging from how safety-relevant behavior actually manifests in deployment. 94% (N=198) do at least acknowledge uncertainty, typically via disclaimers rather than formal uncertainty quantification. In the AIR 2024 case study, the benchmark's most comprehensive comparison alternative covers only 71% of its own level-3 regulatory risk taxonomy, its headline metric (89% refusal rate) is reported as a point estimate with no confidence interval, and its LLM-judge (GPT-4o) validation reports Cohen's kappa of 0.86 against human annotation.
core_contribution: The paper is a scoping review/meta-analysis of 210 AI safety benchmarks (not a new benchmark or attack/defense method). It identifies three structural failure modes in how the field benchmarks AI safety -- (1) imbalanced construct coverage (benchmarks overwhelmingly test 'known known' risks and neglect emergent/unforeseen failure modes), (2) non-rigorous risk quantification (safety reduced to binary pass/fail rates presented as if they were calibrated probabilities, with severity largely unaddressed), and (3) erosion of measurement validity through long proxy chains (e.g., refusal rate or toxicity score standing in for real-world harm without justified linkage). Drawing on safety engineering (probabilistic risk assessment, functional safety/SIL thresholds), the Rumsfeld known/unknown matrix, and measurement theory, the authors derive ten concrete recommendations (R1-R10) grouped under the three failure modes, provide a benchmark design checklist, and apply the framework as a case study to critique the AIR 2024 regulatory-alignment benchmark (Zeng et al., 2024) against the checklist.
threat_model: None -- this is not an attack/defense paper and defines no adversary or threat model. Its unit of analysis is the safety-benchmarking practice/artifact itself (i.e., a meta-level critique of measurement methodology), not a system under attack.
limitations: The review itself does not fully specify (in the paper) the inclusion/exclusion criteria or search methodology used to arrive at the 210-benchmark corpus, nor a formal inter-coder reliability statistic for the authors' own coding of those 210 benchmarks (unlike the AIR 2024 case study, where the paper does report the benchmark's own kappa of 0.86). The recommendations are qualitative/prescriptive (a checklist) rather than validated empirically against improved safety outcomes -- the paper's own 'quantitative...evaluation' claim in the abstract appears to rest on an illustrative Fermi/order-of-magnitude calculation (App. C) rather than a controlled comparison. The authors acknowledge that resource-intensive recommendations (e.g., system-level, community-participatory evaluation) could exacerbate resource inequalities across the research community, and that graded severity scales/safety margins risk 'premature standardization or co-optation for compliance theater.' The paper is explicitly framed as methodological guidance rather than a new benchmark, dataset, or defense.

datasets_used: Corpus of 210 AI safety benchmarks (author-collected scoping-review corpus; individual benchmarks cited include TruthfulQA, MACHIAVELLI, HarmBench, GPTFuzz, WildTeaming, CARNOVEL, MEDFAIR, AIR 2024)
quantitative_results: 81% (N=170) of 210 benchmarks cover only known-known risks
34% (N=72) of benchmarks explicitly document excluded/blind-spot risk types
79% (N=166) of benchmarks rely on binary pass/fail rates as primary/sole metric
36 of 210 benchmarks use graded severity scales; only 14 of those 36 justify the scale on principled grounds
38% (N=79) of benchmarks ground metric definitions in an external framework/regulation/standard
94% (N=198) of benchmarks acknowledge uncertainty (mostly via disclaimers)
89% (N=186) of benchmarks evaluate on fixed data without documenting stochasticity sources
68% (N=143) of benchmarks rely on isolated single-turn interactions
AIR 2024 case study: taxonomy coverage of most comprehensive comparator benchmark = 71% of level-3 regulatory risk categories
AIR 2024 case study: headline refusal rate = 89% (reported as point estimate, no confidence interval)
AIR 2024 case study: human-vs-LLM-judge (GPT-4o) agreement Cohen's kappa = 0.86
Illustrative Fermi calculation (App. C): estimated annual liability for a medium-sized platform on the order of $10^4 under typical severity assumptions
baselines_compared: No ML model baselines are compared; the paper instead evaluates the AIR 2024 benchmark (Zeng et al., 2024) as a case study against its own proposed ten-item (R1-R10) checklist
